content: By 2 months, infants should start smiling at people, briefly calming themselves, trying to look at parents, cooing and gurgling sounds, and turning head toward sounds. They can hold head up and begin to push up when lying on tummy.

By 4 months, infants copy movements and facial expressions, smile spontaneously, like to play with people, babble, cry differently for different needs, and reach for toys with one hand. They can hold head steady without support and may be able to roll over.

By 6 months, infants know familiar faces, like to play with others, respond to own name, make sounds to show joy and displeasure, and look at themselves in mirror. They sit with support and can pass things from one hand to another.

By 9 months, infants may show fear of strangers, have favorite toys, understand "no", make different sounds like "mamamama", and point to show things. They can sit without support, crawl, and pull to stand.

By 12 months, infants show shyness with strangers, cry when parent leaves, have favorite things and people, play games like peek-a-boo, and use simple gestures like waving bye-bye. They can take first steps and say "mama" and "dada".

Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first 6 months of life. Breast milk provides all necessary nutrients and antibodies for the infant. If breastfeeding is not possible, iron-fortified infant formula is the recommended alternative.

At 6 months, introduce solid foods while continuing breastfeeding. Start with iron-fortified single-grain cereals, pureed vegetables, and fruits. Introduce one new food at a time, waiting 3-5 days to watch for allergic reactions.

By 8-12 months, infants can have mashed or finely chopped foods. Offer finger foods like small pieces of banana, crackers, and cooked vegetables. Avoid honey until 12 months due to botulism risk. Avoid cow milk as main drink until 12 months.

Iron is critical for infant brain development. Iron stores from birth deplete by 4-6 months. Iron-rich foods include iron-fortified cereals, pureed meats, beans, and leafy greens. Vitamin C helps iron absorption.

For infants 0-6 months, typical intake is 60-90ml per feed, 6-8 feeds per day. Watch for feeding cues like rooting and sucking on hands. Burp baby during and after feeds. Feeding patterns vary - follow babys hunger cues rather than strict schedules.

WHO growth standards: Average newborn weight is 3.2-3.4kg. Infants typically lose 5-10% of birth weight in first few days, then regain by day 10-14. Weight should double by 4-5 months and triple by 12 months.

WHO growth standards: Average newborn length is 48-53cm. Length increases about 2.5cm per month for first 3 months, then about 1.25cm per month until 6 months. By 12 months, typical length is about 75cm.

WHO growth standards: Average newborn head circumference is 33-35cm. Head circumference grows rapidly in first year - about 2cm per month for first 3 months, then about 1cm per month. This reflects rapid brain development.

Growth percentiles show how an infant compares to peers. Percentiles between 5th and 95th are generally normal. Consistent tracking on same percentile is more important than the specific percentile. Crossing percentiles may indicate issues.

Newborns sleep 14-17 hours per day in 2-4 hour stretches. No day-night rhythm established yet. Safe sleep: Always place baby on back to sleep, use firm flat surface, keep soft objects and loose bedding out of sleep area.

At 3-6 months, infants sleep 12-16 hours per day. Begin developing regular sleep schedule. May start sleeping longer stretches at night (4-6 hours). Establish bedtime routine: bath, feeding, story, dim lights.

By 6-12 months, infants sleep 12-16 hours with 2-3 naps during day. Many can sleep through the night (6-8 hours). Sleep regression may occur around 8-10 months due to developmental milestones and separation anxiety.

Vaccination schedule (WHO): Birth - BCG, Hepatitis B. 6 weeks - DTP, Polio, Hib, Hepatitis B, PCV. 10 weeks - DTP, Polio, Hib, PCV. 14 weeks - DTP, Polio, Hib, PCV. 9 months - Measles. 12-15 months - MMR. Consult local immunization schedule.

Warning signs requiring immediate medical attention: fever above 38C in infant under 3 months, difficulty breathing, persistent vomiting, diarrhea with dehydration signs, lethargy or unusual sleepiness, seizures, rash with fever.

Teething typically begins around 6 months. First teeth are usually lower central incisors. Symptoms include drooling, irritability, mild temperature elevation. Provide teething rings, gentle gum massage. Avoid teething gels with benzocaine.

Tummy time: Essential for developing neck, shoulder, and arm muscles. Start with 2-3 minutes, 2-3 times daily. Gradually increase to 15-30 minutes total. Use rolled towel under chest, get down at babys level, use mirrors for engagement.

Sensory play for development: Provide varied textures (soft cloth, crinkly paper, smooth plastic). Use high-contrast images for newborns. Sing, talk, and read to baby. Rattles and soft toys for grasping. Safe mirror play for self-recognition.

Language development: Talk to baby constantly, narrate daily activities. Read books together daily. Sing songs and nursery rhymes. Respond to babys coos and babbles. Use parentese (higher pitch, exaggerated intonation).

Colic: Excessive crying in otherwise healthy baby, usually starting at 2-4 weeks, peaking at 6 weeks. Typically resolves by 3-4 months. Try: white noise, gentle motion, swaddling, frequent burping, feeding position changes. Consult doctor if concerned.

Reflux: Common in infants due to immature digestive system. Symptoms include spitting up, irritability during/after feeds. Most resolve by 12-18 months. Management: smaller frequent feeds, upright position after feeding, avoid overfeeding.

Developmental delay warning signs: No response to sounds by 3-4 months, not tracking objects by 3 months, not smiling at people by 2 months, not babbling by 6 months, not sitting with support by 8 months, not crawling by 12 months. Consult pediatrician.

Poshan Abhiyan (National Nutrition Mission): Government program to improve nutritional status of children, pregnant women, and lactating mothers. Aims to reduce stunting, undernutrition, anemia. Provides supplementary nutrition at Anganwadi centers.

Mission Vatsalya: Supports child protection services, adoption, foster care. Provides financial assistance to children in need of care and protection. Juvenile Justice implementation. Helpline: Childline 1098.

Pradhan Mantri Matru Vandana Yojana (PMMVY): Cash incentive of Rs.5000 for first living child to pregnant and lactating mothers. Compensates for wage loss, promotes health and nutrition. Apply through Anganwadi centers or online.

Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS): Provides supplementary nutrition, immunization, health check-ups, preschool education at Anganwadi centers. Available to children 0-6 years, pregnant and nursing mothers. Free service.
